verses.text: Naaman, Syria's great commander, is a leper, and a captive Israelite maid tells his wife of a prophet who could heal him. The mighty man's hope comes through a little slave girl's witness. God uses the lowliest and most unlikely to point the great toward His grace.

Naaman, Syria's great commander, is a leper, and a captive Israelite maid tells his wife of a prophet who could heal him. The mighty man's hope comes through a little slave girl's witness. God uses the lowliest and most unlikely to point the great toward His grace.

Naaman, Syria's great commander, is a leper, and a captive Israelite maid tells his wife of a prophet who could heal him. The mighty man's hope comes through a little slave girl's witness. God uses the lowliest and most unlikely to point the great toward His grace.

Naaman, Syria's great commander, is a leper, and a captive Israelite maid tells his wife of a prophet who could heal him. The mighty man's hope comes through a little slave girl's witness. God uses the lowliest and most unlikely to point the great toward His grace.

Naaman comes with silver, gold, and a king's letter, and Israel's king tears his clothes in panic — 'am I God?' He sees only a political trap. When men forget God's prophet is among them, even a chance for His glory looks like a threat.

Naaman comes with silver, gold, and a king's letter, and Israel's king tears his clothes in panic — 'am I God?' He sees only a political trap. When men forget God's prophet is among them, even a chance for His glory looks like a threat.

Naaman comes with silver, gold, and a king's letter, and Israel's king tears his clothes in panic — 'am I God?' He sees only a political trap. When men forget God's prophet is among them, even a chance for His glory looks like a threat.

Elisha sends word: go wash in Jordan seven times and be clean. No ceremony, no fee, no fanfare — just a simple command. God's way of cleansing is humbling in its plainness, and offends those who expect something grand.

Elisha sends word: go wash in Jordan seven times and be clean. No ceremony, no fee, no fanfare — just a simple command. God's way of cleansing is humbling in its plainness, and offends those who expect something grand.

Elisha sends word: go wash in Jordan seven times and be clean. No ceremony, no fee, no fanfare — just a simple command. God's way of cleansing is humbling in its plainness, and offends those who expect something grand.

Naaman rages: he expected the prophet to come out and wave his hand; are not Syria's rivers better than muddy Jordan? Pride nearly costs him his healing. We often despise God's simple remedy because it wounds our dignity and is not what we imagined.

Naaman rages: he expected the prophet to come out and wave his hand; are not Syria's rivers better than muddy Jordan? Pride nearly costs him his healing. We often despise God's simple remedy because it wounds our dignity and is not what we imagined.

His servants reason gently: if the prophet had asked a great thing, would you not have done it? So Naaman dips seven times and his flesh becomes as a child's. Humbling obedience to the plain word brings the cleansing that pride would have forfeited.

His servants reason gently: if the prophet had asked a great thing, would you not have done it? So Naaman dips seven times and his flesh becomes as a child's. Humbling obedience to the plain word brings the cleansing that pride would have forfeited.

Healed, Naaman returns confessing there is no God but Israel's, and presses gifts on Elisha, who utterly refuses them. The prophet will take no pay for God's free grace. Salvation cannot be bought, and God's servants must not let it seem so.

Healed, Naaman returns confessing there is no God but Israel's, and presses gifts on Elisha, who utterly refuses them. The prophet will take no pay for God's free grace. Salvation cannot be bought, and God's servants must not let it seem so.

Naaman asks for Israelite soil to worship the true God in Syria, and pardon when his master leans on him in Rimmon's temple; Elisha says, 'Go in peace.' A new convert's faith is real though still mixed with weakness. God meets the beginner with patience, not a checklist.

Naaman asks for Israelite soil to worship the true God in Syria, and pardon when his master leans on him in Rimmon's temple; Elisha says, 'Go in peace.' A new convert's faith is real though still mixed with weakness. God meets the beginner with patience, not a checklist.

Gehazi, greedy, runs after Naaman with a lie, asking silver and garments in Elisha's name. He turns the free gift of God into private gain. To trade on holy things for personal profit corrupts the very grace one claims to serve.

Gehazi, greedy, runs after Naaman with a lie, asking silver and garments in Elisha's name. He turns the free gift of God into private gain. To trade on holy things for personal profit corrupts the very grace one claims to serve.

Elisha, knowing all, says Naaman's leprosy will cling to Gehazi and his seed — and he goes out white as snow. The sin he thought hidden is exposed and judged. What we grasp by covetous deceit becomes a curse; God is not mocked by His servants.
